text: Hello, I'm sharing another robotic sigmoid with transrectal extraction of specimen and some anastomosis. It's a patient with favorable body habitus. The patient had had several attacks of diverticulitis. The most recent one was about five months prior. Believe it or not, there was an abscess. It did not require drainage, just antibiotics.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, focusing on the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The IMA is skeletonized, creating a window behind it, and then divided using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection towards the sidewall, following the white line interface. A sponge is placed underneath to protect the ureter before proceeding with lateral dissection. The dissection progresses smoothly without challenges, maintaining traction to enter the previously dissected medial plane.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and psoas tendon within the medially dissected plane, proceeding with lateral mobilization towards the splenic flexure. Dissection continues off the left pelvic sidewall down to the upper rectum, aiding mobility for the anastomosis. The surgeon selects the distal transection margin and searches for the proximal margin.

The surgeon clears the distal upper rectal mesentery and identifies the area for the proximal margin, located just cephalad to the transection of the inferior mesenteric artery. The mesentery is taken up towards the proximal margin, and perfusion is assessed using ICG. The surgeon then proceeds to transect the bowel distally and proximally.

When specimens are too large to extract as a whole piece, I will then chop it up into smaller pieces. I have never really found much success with the placement of a transrectal wound protector. I have seen many demonstrated videos of this, which are absolutely beautiful. I just haven't had the same success myself. Additionally, I'm working without residents and I don't have a separate person to go down below, so I'm doing all of this myself. And I just found this to be the easiest way for me to do it. I'm usually transecting specimens, taking the mesentery off when I've done a high ligation. and then transecting the specimen into three or four smaller pieces. The whole thing takes maybe ten minutes. Granted, if you're able to take it out all in one piece through a wound protector, you definitely save time. Now, a relatively unedited showing of one of my hand-sewn single layer anastomosis, which is the way that I'm doing These end to end anastomosis these days. I take a three oh silk suture on the far left lateral side. This is simply to give myself something to hold on to to elevate the anastomosis and for traction and manipulation. There are lots of ways to do this. This is just simply the way I like to. Now I'm starting with a three OB lock in the posterior midline and I usually suture out towards the left side first.

I can individually place these sutures and pull to the tension that I like, which is the explicit reason why I really like these sewn anastomosis and not relying on surgical staplers. I have zero evidence to back up my statement. This part of the procedure certainly takes longer than stapling. I would say that this takes me between twenty and twenty-five minutes these days. What you're saving in time is not having to make a fan and steel incision and then a fan and steel closure. Especially if you are still doing extracorporeal anastomosis stapled, you'd be undocking the robot and then redocking or finishing laparoscopically. So it's kind of time neutral. I'm hopeful that I will get faster with my anastomosis though.

The surgeon begins with a three OB lock suture in the midline and proceeds to stitch towards the near side, creating an over-and-over pattern on the posterior row. As the suture reaches the anterior side, the technique transitions to an inverting Limbert suture. The surgeon adjusts the original suture to avoid the confluence of sutures near a corner.

The surgeon inverts the two lips of mucosa and ties the sutures together to complete the anastomosis. A leak test is performed by flooding the pelvis and inserting a rigid scope below the anastomosis, showing no bubbles and confirming the anastomosis is widely patent.
